chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove, next is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, which is clearly the first app-level native function below the allocator/runtime.

Given failure in memmove, most plausible root cause is out-of-bounds read/write or overlapping buffers with incorrect length, driven by how BSON bytes are parsed in byte_array_to_bson_string / bson_*_from_bytes.

The JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes takes a Java byte[] and length and hands it to the BSON parser. Under fuzzing, arbitrary byte sequences reached memmove with no sanitizer/bounds checks visible in the call chain.

BSON decoders must walk a length-prefixed binary format, trusting embedded size fields. If these fields are not fully validated, malformed data can cause the parser to compute wrong copy lengths and pass them to memmove, triggering the crash seen under fuzzing.

Because the fuzz harness calls exactly the same JNI entry that production code uses, and inputs are just arbitrary byte arrays (which real code can obtain from network/files), this path is realistic and not an artifact of the harness (no impossible JNI signatures or forced NULLs indicated).

Even though I couldn't decompile the specific functions due to tool limitations (404s), the call stack structure (byte_array_to_bson_string → bson_array_from_bytes → bson_object_from_bytes → memmove) is typical of a deserializer using memmove/memcpy on attacker-influenced data without complete length validation.

Therefore, this is likely a genuine, attacker-triggerable memory corruption bug in libbson-c-lib, reachable whenever the app decodes untrusted BSON (e.g., from a remote source). Exploitability is at least theoretical and probably practical (heap corruption → potential RCE), but crash details don't show exact overwrite direction/controls, so severity is marked high with ~0.7 confidence.

Env/harness explanation is unlikely because: (1) the bridge method matches real app JNI, (2) the library map shows a normal production BSON parser, not a synthetic harness-only entry, and (3) memmove misuse under malformed-format input is a textbook parser bug surface.
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove after parsing attacker-controllable BSON bytes via byte_array_to_bson_string / bson_*_from_bytes.

JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is a real app entry that accepts a raw byte array, so malformed data from network/files can realistically reach the vulnerable code.

BSON parsing is known to be length/offset-driven; without strong evidence of comprehensive bounds checks, an out-of-bounds memmove in this path is best explained by a memory-corruption bug, not by harness misuse.
recommendations: Manually review byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_array_from_bytes, and bson_object_from_bytes for all memmove/memcpy/memset calls and ensure lengths are derived only from validated bounds-checked values.
Add rigorous validation of all BSON length/offset fields before using them in pointer arithmetic or as memmove sizes; reject inputs on any overflow, underflow, or range violation.
Enable hardened allocators and address sanitizers in debug builds to reproduce and localize the exact out-of-bounds access and confirm fix.
assumptions: The JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is used in normal app flows to parse potentially untrusted BSON (e.g., from network or disk).
No comprehensive bounds checks completely sanitize all length and offset fields before reaching memmove in byte_array_to_bson_string.
The fuzz harness uses the same calling convention and argument constraints as production Java code, other than supplying arbitrary bytes.
limitations: Ghidra MCP search_functions_by_name calls for the specific functions failed with HTTP 404, so I could not inspect exact decompiled code or argument expressions.
The exact cause at the memmove site (overlong length vs. bad pointer vs. overlap) is unknown, so exploitability is inferred from context rather than proven.
Java-side call graph into Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes was not inspected, so precise attacker-control sources (which Activities/Intents) remain unidentified.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted BSON byte array to any app feature that calls BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (or equivalent) and passes it into the JNI bridge.
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse attacker-controlled BSON bytes (e.g., via network API, IPC, or file import).
Target device runs a build without full memory-safety mitigations (typical production Android).
exploit_pipeline: Identify an app feature or API that accepts or constructs BSON data and internally calls BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes, reaching Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes.

Craft a malicious BSON payload with inconsistent length fields or nested structures designed to cause bson_object_from_bytes / bson_array_from_bytes to compute a memmove length or pointer that goes out of bounds.
Deliver the crafted BSON to the vulnerable feature (e.g., via network request body, imported file, or IPC message) so that it is passed unchanged as a byte[] into the JNI bridge.

Observe process crash or memory corruption when memmove is invoked with the invalid size; iterate payload to attempt controlled overwrite patterns for advanced exploitation (e.g., heap metadata or adjacent objects).
exploit.notes: Exact Java entry (class/method) is inferred from the JNI name (com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes); you must locate the corresponding Java API in the decompiled app and adapt the PoC to the actual data ingestion path.

chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame below allocator: crash occurs in __memcpy, next frame is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so. This is the first relevant application-level function.
Given the provided data, I cannot actually decompile with Ghidra tools or inspect Java with Jadx for this specific project, so I must reason conceptually from the symbol names and stack structure.

Function names strongly suggest a typical pattern: Java passes a byte[] to JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes, which then calls bson_object_from_bytes, which calls bson_array_from_bytes recursively, which finally uses byte_array_to_bson_string. Inside byte_array_to_bson_string, __memcpy is called and causes the crash.

A memcpy-based crash almost always indicates a length/size mismatch: either the destination buffer is too small or the source pointer/length is inconsistent with actual backing memory. Given the stack trace, the memcpy is clearly in app/library code, not in allocator or harness-only logic.

Because this is a JNI fuzzing harness, the fuzzer can arbitrarily control the byte[] payload passed to the JNI entry point. That means any parsing of BSON structures that relies on embedded lengths or counts is influenced by attacker-controlled input.

BSON parsing typically reads length fields from the data and then allocates or indexes into buffers based on those lengths. If these lengths are not fully validated against the actual remaining buffer size, a malformed or adversarial payload can cause out-of-bounds reads or writes during memcpy. The recursive bson_array_from_bytes and byte_array_to_bson_string names reinforce that the code walks nested structures and copies strings/arrays based on encoded lengths.

The crash is in __memcpy, not in an explicit sanity-check or abort. This suggests an actual memory access violation rather than a deliberate defensive check. JNI fuzzing harness issues (e.g., passing NULL for mandatory buffers) typically produce null derefs or argument checks, not a deep recursive traversal leading to memcpy.

There is no indication of harness-only behavior (like passing a length that normal Java code never would). The JNI bridge method name suggests it directly exposes a “from_bytes” API that would legitimately be used with arbitrary serialized data (e.g., from network, disk, or IPC) in the real app, so untrusted input driving this path is plausible in production.

Given the function naming, there is almost certainly a memcpy inside byte_array_to_bson_string that copies data based on a size derived from the BSON payload without full bounds checking. That is a classic out-of-bounds read/write scenario (CWE-787/CWE-119).

Without the exact decompiled snippet, I cannot prove whether the overflow is on source or destination; however, either case is a memory safety vulnerability. The involvement of nested arrays/objects indicates that crafted length fields can produce very large or inconsistent lengths leading to overflow or out-of-bounds conditions.

Impact: library is native C, running in app process. Memory corruption via unsafe memcpy is generally exploitable for code execution under favorable conditions or at least a serious DoS. Given limited insight into ASLR/allocators and structure, classify severity as high rather than critical, but note that RCE cannot be excluded.
Therefore, I assess this crash as likely a real vulnerability (unsafe memcpy in BSON parsing reachable via untrusted bytes), not a harness-only artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from byte_array_to_bson_string within libbson-c-lib.so, indicating an unsafe memory copy in app/library code.

JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes takes attacker-controlled byte[] and passes it through multiple BSON parsing functions, so sizes/offsets used in memcpy are ultimately derived from untrusted input.

Function naming (bson_array_from_bytes, bson_object_from_bytes, byte_array_to_bson_string) strongly suggests typical length-driven parsing of serialized data, where malformed length fields can cause out-of-bounds memory access.
The crash is deep in parsing logic rather than at JNI boundary or obvious harness misuse, making it unlikely to be purely an environment/harness artifact.
Native memory corruption via incorrect memcpy length is a well-known, potentially exploitable vulnerability pattern.
recommendations: Review and harden BSON parsing functions (bson_object_from_bytes, bson_array_from_bytes, byte_array_to_bson_string) to validate all length and offset fields against the actual buffer size before calling memcpy.
Add centralized bounds-checking helpers for BSON element lengths (strings, arrays, documents) and ensure all callers use them before allocations or copies.
Compile libbson-c-lib with sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) and run targeted fuzzing on BSON decoding APIs to reproduce and precisely localize the failing memcpy call.
assumptions: The byte[] passed into Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes in production can originate from untrusted sources (e.g., network, file, IPC).
The crashing __memcpy call uses a length derived, directly or indirectly, from the parsed BSON payload, not from a fixed constant or fully validated size.
There are no comprehensive bounds checks in the BSON parsing stack that would prevent invalid lengths from reaching byte_array_to_bson_string.
limitations: Exact decompiled code for byte_array_to_bson_string and related functions is not available, so the precise overflow direction (source vs destination) is unknown.
Java-side call graph and argument validation for BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes could not be inspected directly; assessment assumes minimal or no additional length validation in Java.
No register/memory dump from the crash is available, preventing confirmation of exact length values or pointer validity at the memcpy site.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted BSON byte sequence to the BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes JNI-backed API
prerequisites: Attacker can provide or influence BSON-encoded data processed by the app (e.g., via network, file, or IPC).
The app uses BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (or a wrapper) on this untrusted data.
Standard Android protections (ASLR, RELRO, etc.) are present but do not fully prevent exploiting a heap/stack corruption in this context.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON document with inconsistent or extreme length fields for strings/arrays/documents so that internal parsing computes an incorrect copy length.
Deliver this crafted BSON payload to the target app via a reachable channel that ultimately calls BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes.
When the app processes the payload, bson_object_from_bytes recursively calls bson_array_from_bytes and then byte_array_to_bson_string.
Inside byte_array_to_bson_string, the malformed length causes memcpy to read/write out of bounds, corrupting memory or causing a crash.
Iteratively refine the payload to control the pattern and location of memory corruption for potential code execution, or at minimum achieve a reliable denial-of-service crash.

# Assumes a placeholder activity and file path; adjust to real values once known
adb push crafted_bson.bin /sdcard/Download/payload.bson
adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity> --es bson_path /sdcard/Download/payload.bson
exploit.notes: A working PoC requires identifying the exact Java entry point that wraps BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (e.g., a deserialization helper). Then feed a minimally malformed BSON document that reproduces the memcpy crash under non-fuzzing conditions and iteratively tune element lengths to steer memory corruption.

chain_of_thought: Stack trace shows crash in memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string -> bson_object_from_bytes -> JNI. This pattern strongly suggests a buffer move/copy overrun based on attacker-controlled length derived from input bytes interpreted as BSON.

GHIDRA search by function name failed (404), but library and symbol names indicate standard BSON encoding implementation; byte_array_to_bson_string likely converts a Java byte[] to internal string/buffer, and bson_object_from_bytes parses the raw BSON object.

The fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input presumably passes arbitrary byte sequences to the JNI method, modeling untrusted Java-level input. Since JNI method name Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes directly exposes this functionality, production Java can pass network or file data here.

memmove aborts when given invalid arguments (overlapping incorrectly sized regions or size exceeding allocation) pointing to insufficient validation of length fields or structure size when decoding BSON. This is not a harness-only issue like NULL misuse; it is intrinsic to how arbitrary bytes are interpreted.

Given typical BSON parsing, length fields inside the buffer control how many bytes are copied into local structures or strings. If code trusts these fields without cross-checking against the overall buffer length, attacker-controlled length can cause out-of-bounds read/write or allocator aborts.
Even though specific decompiled code was unavailable due to tool error, the symbol names and crash pattern provide strong evidence of an unsafe memmove using sizes derived from the input buffer.

The JNI bridge likely just forwards a jbyteArray and its length to bson_object_from_bytes; without explicit bounds checks in the native layer, malformed BSON can crash memmove, leading to a realistic remote/local DoS and possible memory corruption in other builds.

This is therefore a likely real vulnerability, at least a denial-of-service via malformed BSON, with potential for more serious corruption depending on allocator and platform; classify as memory corruption (CWE-787/119 style) with high severity and note some uncertainty from missing exact code.
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove called from BSON parsing helpers, strongly suggesting misuse of length/size derived from input bytes.
Input is attacker-controlled via JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes taking arbitrary byte arrays (fuzzer models untrusted input).
Pattern is consistent with out-of-bounds copy or overlap during BSON string/object decoding (byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_object_from_bytes) without sufficient bounds validation.
No evidence this is purely a harness artifact (no NULL-only misuse or impossible call signature).
recommendations: Decompile and review bson_object_from_bytes and byte_array_to_bson_string to find all memmove/memcpy calls and ensure lengths are validated against the source buffer size before copying.
Add strict bounds checks when parsing BSON (e.g., validate declared string/element lengths against remaining buffer length) and reject malformed input instead of copying blindly.
Integrate ASAN/UBSAN builds and targeted fuzzing of BSON inputs around these functions to confirm and harden fixes.
assumptions: JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is reachable from regular app code and operates on untrusted data such as network or file inputs.
byte_array_to_bson_string uses memmove to copy from the provided byte array into an internal buffer or string without sufficient bounds checks.
The fuzz harness mirrors realistic usage by passing raw BSON-like byte arrays.
limitations: GHIDRA MCP search by name failed (404), so specific decompiled code snippets were not inspected.
Exact memmove arguments (src, dst, length) and their provenance could not be traced, leaving some uncertainty about precise corruption semantics.
Java-side call graph and validation logic around the JNI method were not directly inspected; analysis infers typical patterns from naming and structure.

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed BSON byte array passed to Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes via normal app API.
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse attacker-controlled BSON or BSON-like byte arrays (e.g., via network, IPC, or file).
App uses BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (or equivalent) on such data without additional validation.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON byte array with inconsistent or oversized length fields for a string or document so that the parser computes an excessive copy length.
Deliver this malicious byte array to the app via whatever channel uses BsonEncoder (e.g., upload payload, send over network, or place in file that app parses).
Trigger the Java code path that calls BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes, causing the JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes to be invoked with the crafted bytes.

When native code executes bson_object_from_bytes -> byte_array_to_bson_string, memmove is called with an invalid length/region, causing an out-of-bounds operation and process crash (DoS) and potentially memory corruption on some builds.

exploit.notes: With current information, the most reliable observed effect is a process crash (denial-of-service) when parsing malformed BSON. Depending on allocator and build flags, this may be escalated to more serious memory corruption in non-sanitized builds if the out-of-bounds write is exploitable.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below libc is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, calling memmove; crash reason is generic memmove failure, suggesting out-of-bounds or overlap misuse rather than explicit abort.
Tooling failed to decompile/search specific symbols (HTTP 404 from MCP), so I cannot inspect exact C code or argument flow; must rely on stack names and general patterns for BSON encoders.

Call chain is fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes -> bson_object_from_bytes -> byte_array_to_bson_string -> memmove. Fuzzer passes arbitrary byte[] that ultimately drives BSON parsing and string conversion.
Function names strongly imply byte_array_to_bson_string converts a raw BSON-encoded byte array into a BSON string, likely using length fields embedded in the BSON structure to compute copy sizes.

If the code treats length from attacker-controlled BSON (embedded in fuzzed byte[]) as trusted and uses it as memmove length without validating against actual buffer size or array bounds, an out-of-bounds read/write can occur, which matches memmove crash.

This is realistic in production: Java layer exposes bson_object_from_bytes to app code, which may be passed network or file data. Fuzzer merely simulates untrusted bytes; it is not misusing JNI in a clearly unrealistic way (e.g., NULL env or fake pointers).

We lack direct proof (no decompiled snippet), but the combination of (a) structured-data parser, (b) string conversion helper, (c) memmove crash, and (d) untrusted length from input is a classic pattern of length-field bugs in BSON / TLV parsers.

Given these factors, it is likely a genuine memory safety issue (CWE-787/788) or at least an unbounded memcpy leading to DoS; impact is at least high due to memory corruption potential, even if full RCE is not demonstrated.

Uncertainty remains because memmove might also crash on extremely large but still-in-bounds length due to allocator behavior or harness-induced impossible sizes, but that is less likely than a plain out-of-bounds from malformed BSON length.

No evidence that the crash is due solely to harness misuse (e.g., NULL JNI pointers); stack shows plausible production call path

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted BSON byte array to the encoder path that calls bson_object_from_bytes
prerequisites: Attacker can cause application to parse attacker-controlled BSON bytes via BSON.BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes or equivalent
Native library libbson-c-lib.so is present and used in the deployed app
No additional strong validation of BSON lengths is performed before native call
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON buffer with inconsistent or malicious length fields for string/document elements that cause computed copy size to exceed actual buffer or destination
Deliver this buffer to the application through any feature that ultimately calls BSON.BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (e.g., deserialization of received messages or files)
Trigger parsing so that bson_object_from_bytes invokes byte_array_to_bson_string, reaching the vulnerable memmove
On vulnerable devices, achieve memory corruption or at least a controlled native crash (denial of service) when memmove reads/writes out of bounds
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity> --es input_path /sdcard/Download/payload.bson # assumes activity reads file and passes bytes to BSON encoder
exploit.notes: Exact Java entrypoint and Intent extras are unknown; PoC sketch assumes there is a code path that reads a file or network payload into a byte[] and passes it to the JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes.

chain_of_thought: Native crash at __memcpy with preceding frames entirely inside libbson-c-lib.so suggests a buffer copy issue inside BSON processing rather than allocator or harness only.

First app-level native frame below libc is byte_array_to_bson_string, followed by bson_array_from_bytes, bson_object_from_bytes, then the JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes. These are all in the same library and match the library map.

I attempted to decompile these functions via Ghidra MCP (search_functions_by_name) but the backend returned 404 for those queries, so I cannot inspect the exact C code, argument flow, or where memcpy is called. This limits detailed data-flow analysis inside the native library.

Conceptually, bson_*_from_bytes / byte_array_to_bson_string consume a byte array and parse BSON. Crashing in memcpy in such parsers is typically due to trusting encoded length fields or indexes without checking against the actual buffer size, producing out-of-bounds reads/writes.

The JNI bridge name Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes strongly suggests it directly receives a Java byte[] from higher-level Java code and passes it more or less verbatim into bson_object_from_bytes / bson_array_from_bytes, and then into byte_array_to_bson_string. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input simulates arbitrary untrusted bytes here, mimicking attacker-controlled input in production (e.g., network or file data) because the library’s whole purpose is to decode externally supplied BSON.

Even without decompilation, the call pattern and the crash in a raw __memcpy make an unchecked-length copy the most plausible root cause: some internal length derived from the BSON payload (string length, array length, document size) is used as memcpy length without validating it against the remaining buffer or allocation size.

There is no evidence that the crash is due purely to an artificial harness misuse (like passing NULL where Java never would); the stack shows a normal decode path. JNI fuzzers usually hand arbitrary-but-valid pointers/lengths corresponding to Java byte[]; that is exactly what real app code can do with attacker-sourced BSON blobs.

Given this, I infer a likely out-of-bounds read/write or similar memory corruption in BSON parsing logic, triggered by malformed BSON content. That is a classic native parsing vulnerability (CWE-787 / CWE-119).

Impact: at minimum, a reliable native DoS (process crash) whenever malicious BSON is processed. Depending on exact direction and control of the overflow, this could plausibly be turned into code execution, but I lack enough internal detail to assert RCE. I therefore treat severity as high rather than critical, with exploitability rated as theoretical to practical for DoS and maybe more with further analysis.

Limitations: I cannot see exact boundary checks, type of memcpy, whether the overflow is limited to a stack buffer or heap structure, or if sanitizers guard it in production. No Java callgraph was provided beyond the JNI bridge name. However, the fuzz harness entry and the nature of this library make the path clearly reachable with attacker data in realistic scenarios.

Because all observed evidence is consistent with a genuine memory-safety bug in BSON parsing, and not with a pure harness artifact, I classify this as a likely real vulnerability with reasonably high confidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from BSON parsing helpers inside libbson-c-lib.so, indicative of an unsafe buffer copy

JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes takes arbitrary byte arrays (attacker-controlled in practice) and forwards them into bson_*_from_bytes without visible native length validation
Fuzz harness fuzz_one_input simply supplies arbitrary BSON-like data; this matches realistic untrusted input usage, not an artificial impossible state
Out-of-bounds memory access in a binary parser is a well-known, exploitable class of vulnerabilities, at minimum causing a native process crash (DoS)
recommendations: Manually decompile and audit byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_array_from_bytes, and bson_object_from_bytes for all memcpy/memmove usages and ensure lengths are checked against buffer sizes and remaining input bytes
Add strict BSON structural validation (document length, string length, array bounds) before performing any copies, rejecting malformed inputs early

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a malformed BSON byte array to the BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes JNI path
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse attacker-controlled BSON data via BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes or equivalent API
libbson-c-lib.so is used in production build without full memory-safety sanitizers
The vulnerable memcpy is reachable for malformed documents (e.g., invalid length fields) in normal app flows
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON document with inconsistent or oversized length fields in a string, array, or document size so that internal parsing logic computes a memcpy length larger than the available buffer
Deliver this BSON blob to the target app via a feature that uses BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (e.g., network API, local file, or IPC payload)
Ensure the app invokes the JNI method with the crafted byte[] so native code reaches bson_object_from_bytes → bson_array_from_bytes → byte_array_to_bson_string
Trigger the memcpy-based overflow, causing a native crash (DoS) and potentially corrupting adjacent memory for further exploitation
poc_commands:
# Or, in an instrumented test harness:
# adb shell am instrument -w -e BSON_FILE /sdcard/Download/payload.bson <package>/.test/.BsonFuzzRunner
exploit.notes: Exact field layout and overlong length values for the crafted BSON require reverse-engineering of libbson-c-lib.so and observing the failing memcpy arguments, which was not possible via the automated tools here.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame before libc is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so; crash is in __memcpy, which is almost always due to size/overlap issues or invalid pointers. The call stack shows a clear parsing pipeline: fuzz_one_input → Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes → bson_object_from_bytes → bson_array_from_bytes → byte_array_to_bson_string → __memcpy. This strongly suggests that arbitrary bytes from the fuzzer (representing a purported BSON object/array) are being passed into native BSON parsing/encoding functions.

Because the tool calls to decompile/search failed with HTTP 404, I cannot inspect the actual C implementation. However, the naming and usage pattern are highly indicative: byte_array_to_bson_string likely takes a raw byte buffer and length and converts it into an internal BSON string representation, probably using memcpy to copy a claimed length. If that length is read from the BSON data without sufficient bounds checking against the total buffer length, then a malformed BSON document could cause out-of-bounds reads/writes or pass an oversized length into memcpy, leading to the observed crash.

The JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is a public-facing method used by the app to deserialize BSON objects from byte arrays. In normal operation, those bytes are expected to come from app data sources such as network responses, files, or IPC. In a realistic threat model, an attacker controlling those sources could craft malicious BSON documents. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input simply wires arbitrary bytes into this same JNI bridge, confirming that there are no extra internal guards in the JNI layer that would prevent untrusted input from reaching the native parser.

The fact that fuzzing triggered a crash in __memcpy, rather than e.g. a handled parse error, indicates that the native code is not robust against malformed lengths or structures. A correct parser would validate all lengths and offsets before copying. Hitting libc memcpy with invalid parameters is a classic symptom of memory-corruption bugs in parsers. Even though I can't see exactly whether the memcpy is writing out of bounds, reading out of bounds, or using a bad pointer, all three cases are serious and generally exploitable in a C library when attacker-controlled input feeds the length/offset fields.

Given the library name (libbson-c-lib.so) and function names mirroring BSON operations, this appears to be a custom or third-party BSON implementation used by the app. Parser vulnerabilities in such libraries are widely documented and often exploitable for memory corruption. The crash path shows no indication that this is due to an unrealistic harness artifact like a NULL JNIEnv or invalid Java array; instead, it originates from the internal parsing logic operating on supplied bytes. Therefore, this is best classified as a likely memory-safety vulnerability (CWE-787 / CWE-119) with high severity, as it could feasibly be triggered via untrusted BSON data in production.

Limitations: I could not perform the mandated detailed backward data-flow via Ghidra because search/decompile calls failed due to a 404 error, so I must infer behavior from symbol names and stack structure. I also do not know exactly which app entrypoint lets remote data reach this JNI function, but the Java callgraph label and typical BSON usage patterns strongly suggest remote or file-based data paths. As a result, I assign high but not absolute confidence (around 0.85) that this is a genuine vulnerability rather than a harness-only artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from byte_array_to_bson_string within libbson-c-lib.so, indicating misuse of memcpy with attacker-influenced data lengths or pointers.
JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is directly fed by fuzz_one_input and is realistically used to parse externally supplied BSON byte arrays in production.
Lack of graceful parse error handling and direct fall-through into libc memcpy on malformed data suggests inadequate bounds checking in the BSON parser, a classic memory-safety flaw.
Crash is independent of obvious harness artifacts (e.g., NULL JNIEnv, impossible Java object states) and instead arises in internal parsing logic.
recommendations: Audit byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_array_from_bytes, and bson_object_from_bytes for all memcpy/memmove calls and enforce strict bounds checking of lengths and offsets against the input buffer size before copying.
Add robust validation and error handling in the BSON parser so malformed or truncated inputs cause parse errors instead of continuing into memcpy with unchecked lengths.
Introduce defensive mitigations such as size caps on BSON documents from untrusted sources and add ASan/UBSan builds of libbson-c-lib.so during testing to catch additional memory-safety issues.
assumptions: BSON byte arrays reaching Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes can be attacker-controlled via network, file, or IPC in realistic app workflows.
The failing memcpy in byte_array_to_bson_string uses a length or pointer derived directly or indirectly from fields within the BSON input without comprehensive validation.
The fuzz harness accurately mirrors the production JNI usage pattern aside from input source.
limitations: Ghidra MCP decompilation and function search failed with HTTP 404, so I could not inspect the exact memcpy call sites or argument flow.
The exact Java callgraph and Android components that feed data into BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes are not visible, so the remote exploit path is inferred, not proven.
The precise nature of the memory error (out-of-bounds read vs write vs invalid pointer) and its exploitability for code execution cannot be confirmed without binary-level analysis.

exploit.trigger_method: Malicious or malformed BSON byte array passed into the BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes JNI bridge from Java code.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence BSON data consumed by the app (e.g., via network API, downloaded file, or IPC).
The app uses BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (or equivalent Java wrapper) on untrusted data without prior strict validation.
Target device runs a build that includes the vulnerable libbson-c-lib.so version.
exploit_pipeline: Identify an application feature or API that deserializes BSON data through BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes or equivalent JNI-backed methods.
Craft a BSON payload with inconsistent length fields or oversized string/array lengths designed to cause byte_array_to_bson_string to compute an invalid memcpy length or pointer.
Deliver the crafted BSON payload through the identified input channel (network, file, IPC) so that the Java layer passes the bytes directly into the JNI decoder.

When the app processes the payload, the native BSON parser in libbson-c-lib.so reaches byte_array_to_bson_string and executes memcpy with attacker-controlled parameters, resulting in a crash or potential memory corruption exploitable under favorable conditions.
poc_commands: # Alternatively, if there is an exported service or receiver that accepts BSON input, invoke it with a crafted extra or file URI.
exploit.notes: Without precise Java entrypoint names, the PoC uses placeholders for the package and activity. A real PoC would wire crafted_bson.bin into the specific feature that calls BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes. Further binary analysis of byte_array_to_bson_string and surrounding code is required to determine whether the memcpy misuse is directly exploitable beyond denial-of-service.

chain_of_thought: Native crash in __memcpy with preceding frames byte_array_to_bson_string -> bson_object_from_bytes -> JNI bridge; memcpy suggests buffer overread/overwrite or invalid pointer.

Tools failed (404) so I cannot decompile native functions; however, Java side shows decodeFromBytes(byte[]) directly calls native bson_object_from_bytes with attacker-controlled byte[] and no validation on length or contents.

JNI symbol in stack appears as Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes but Java class exposes private static native long bson_object_from_bytes(byte[]), consistent with a JNI bridge taking raw bytes to parse a BSON object.

Crash in __memcpy during byte_array_to_bson_string strongly indicates that the native library walks the provided buffer according to internal BSON length fields and uses memcpy to materialize strings; malformed or inconsistent lengths can cause OOB read/write if library lacks robust bounds checking.

Given this comes from a production BSON encoder/decoder (libbson-c-lib.so) used by decodeFromBytes, real-world callers will hand it untrusted data (e.g., network, IPC) so a memory corruption in memcpy is security-relevant, not a harness artifact.

I cannot prove exact argument taint to memcpy (src/dst/len) due to MCP errors not returning decompilation, but the minimal stack, library naming, and direct byte[] flow from Java through JNI to parser give strong evidence of a genuine parser bug triggered by malformed BSON.

Thus classify as likely vulnerability (OOB read/write in BSON string conversion) with high but not full certainty; severity high because of potential for code execution or at least process compromise, though exact exploitability unknown.

recommendations: Manually audit byte_array_to_bson_string and related BSON string parsing paths for all memcpy/memmove calls to ensure lengths are validated against the actual buffer bounds

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted BSON byte array to decodeFromBytes(byte[]) so that native parsing computes out-of-bounds memcpy length
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the byte[] passed into com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes
Target app processes untrusted BSON-like data using this library
Standard Android memory protections (ASLR, NX) are in place, so exploitation may require heap grooming and info leaks
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON payload whose internal length fields for a string or binary value cause the parser to compute a memcpy length larger than the real buffer segment or object
Deliver this payload through the app feature that ultimately calls BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes (e.g., network message, file, IPC extras)
When decodeFromBytes invokes native bson_object_from_bytes, the parser reaches byte_array_to_bson_string and calls memcpy with the malicious length, causing out-of-bounds access
Iteratively refine the payload (adjusting sizes and structure) to attempt to gain controlled overwrite or read of adjacent heap memory and observe resulting crashes or behavior
poc_commands:
# Or, if a test harness exists:
adb shell am instrument -w -e BSON_INPUT /sdcard/Download/payload.bson <package>/.test.BsonFuzzRunner
exploit.notes: Because the exact app entrypoint consuming BSON is unknown, the PoC uses placeholder activity and instrumentation names; a tester should map these to the real components using the manifest and code paths leading to BsonEncoder.decodeFromBytes().

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, next is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so; that is the first app-level native frame below a libc primitive.
I cannot decompile here due to tool backend error, but memcpy crashes almost always indicate an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap issue, driven by an incorrect length argument or bad pointer.
The function name byte_array_to_bson_string plus following bson_object_from_bytes strongly suggests this code decodes a BSON document from a caller-provided byte array.

The JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes exposes this functionality directly to Java. The fuzz harness passes arbitrary bytes into this JNI, mimicking attacker-controlled data in realistic scenarios (e.g., network/file/IPC BSON payloads).

Given typical BSON decoders, they often read the BSON size prefix from the first 4 bytes and then trust it to allocate or to memcpy; if the input is shorter than claimed size or malformed, a length miscalculation can lead to memcpy overrunning either the input buffer or a destination buffer.

No evidence indicates the crash is due only to the harness (e.g., passing a null env or invalid JNI reference). The stack is clean: fuzz_one_input -> JNI -> decoding helpers -> memcpy. This is consistent with a real parsing bug rather than an environmental misconfiguration.

Even if crash were an out-of-bounds read only, it is at least a robustness/DoS issue. But memcpy and BSON parsing make an out-of-bounds write quite plausible, implying memory corruption and potential RCE under the right conditions.

Without code we cannot name exact variables, but the logical chain (attacker-controlled byte array -> bson_object_from_bytes -> byte_array_to_bson_string -> memcpy crash) demonstrates attacker influence over the memcpy length or pointers.
Therefore, this should be classified as a likely, but not fully confirmed, memory-corruption vulnerability in libbson-c-lib, triggered by malformed BSON bytes from Java.

chain_of_thought: Native crash occurs in memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string inside libbson-c-lib.so, with a clear BSON-related parsing context.

The top relevant app-level native frame below the C library is byte_array_to_bson_string; other frames (bson_array_from_bytes, bson_object_from_bytes, JNI bridge) indicate this is decoding attacker-controlled byte arrays into BSON objects.

Even though Ghidra MCP search for symbols failed (404), function names and stack trace are descriptive enough to infer semantics: BSON decoder functions are operating on a Java-provided byte[] without guaranteed bounds checks inside native code.

A memmove crash in such a decoder strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap issue where the computed length for memmove exceeds the backing buffer derived from the input, rather than harness-only misuse (no evidence of the fuzzer violating JNI contracts beyond providing arbitrary bytes).

The JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes exposes this path directly to Java; typical usage in apps is to pass user/network-derived data. Fuzz harness mirrors this realistic call by feeding arbitrary byte arrays, showing that malformed BSON can trigger unsafe memmove in byte_array_to_bson_string.

Given memmove is crashing (likely due to invalid src/dst/len), this is classic memory corruption / invalid memory access in a parser on untrusted input, fitting CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write) and/or CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read).

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed BSON byte[] passed through com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes JNI bridge
prerequisites: Attacker can influence or supply BSON byte arrays processed by the app (e.g., via network payload or file)
App uses com.livio.BSON.BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes to parse untrusted data
Target device allows installation or interaction with the vulnerable app
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON-like byte sequence with fields/lengths designed to cause miscalculated sizes in byte_array_to_bson_string leading to invalid memmove arguments
Deliver this payload through an app feature that uses BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (e.g., network message, imported file)
Trigger the parsing functionality in the app so that the payload is passed to the JNI method
On processing, the native libbson-c-lib.so executes memmove with out-of-bounds src/dst/len, causing a crash and potentially controllable memory corruption
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity> --es bson_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_bson.bin  # assumes activity that loads and parses file
exploit.notes: Exact field layout of crafted_bson.bin must be tuned by experimenting with BSON lengths and nested arrays/objects to drive byte_array_to_bson_string into its vulnerable memmove path.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame before allocator is memmove, next is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so; crash reason suggests memmove misuse (likely OOB read/write) inside that helper.

I attempted to locate and decompile byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_object_from_bytes, bson_array_from_bytes, and the JNI method in Ghidra via search_functions_by_name, but the MCP endpoint returned 404 errors, so I cannot inspect the actual C code or data flow into memmove.

Without decompiled code, I cannot trace src/dst/len back to JNI parameters; however, the stack shows a clear app-level helper (byte_array_to_bson_string) immediately under memmove, so the memmove call is in app/library code rather than just a libc wrapper crash.

The JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes indicates this is part of a BSON encoding/decoding library, taking a Java byte[] and turning it into a BSON object; by design, the fuzz harness is feeding arbitrary bytes into this JNI, which in production would be partially attacker-controlled (e.g., from network or storage).

A well-written parser must treat incoming byte[] as untrusted; a crash in memmove while converting an untrusted byte array to a BSON string strongly suggests an out-of-bounds memory access or length miscalculation rather than a deliberate explicit abort (no abort/LOG frames are present).

Because memmove is crashing, either dst or src pointer is invalid or length is too large; this is typically caused by missing bounds checks or integer overflow during size computation, both of which are memory-safety vulnerabilities (CWE-787/805).

There is no evidence this is a harness-only misuse (e.g., calling JNI with impossible preconditions); the JNI method is a normal exported symbol in libbson-c-lib.so and part of the real app, and the name indicates it should be robust against arbitrary BSON bytes, so a crash here under malformed input is still a vulnerability (parser DoS / memory corruption).

Due to lack of decompiled code I cannot pinpoint a specific arithmetic bug or prove write-vs-read corruption, but the combination of: (1) low-level memmove crash, (2) user-controlled byte[] path via JNI, and (3) absence of any validation/abort frames yields moderate confidence that this is a genuine memory-safety bug in the BSON parsing logic.

Impact-wise, the worst case is heap corruption leading to code execution (critical), but with only a memmove crash and no heap exploitation evidence, a conservative rating is high severity (memory corruption / reliable DoS from malformed input).
Given these factors, I classify this as a likely vulnerability stemming from unsafe memmove use in byte_array_to_bson_string / BSON decoding of untrusted bytes.
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove immediately under app helper byte_array_to_bson_string, indicating misuse of memmove in library code rather than a benign abort

recommendations: Reverse-engineer and audit byte_array_to_bson_string and related BSON parsing routines for all memmove/memcpy/memset calls, ensuring lengths are derived from validated buffer sizes and cannot exceed allocated regions
Add robust bounds checks and graceful error handling for malformed/undersized BSON input in both Java and JNI layers, returning parse errors instead of proceeding to memmove
